title: Witch Week - Chapter 1 (Fragment 4)
text:
When Charles wrote games, he meant bad luck. Like everyone else in 6B, he had seen Mr. Crossley had found a note. He did not know what was in the note, but when he looked up and caught Mr. Crossley’s eye, he knew it meant bad luck coming.

Mr. Crossley still could not decide what to do about the note. If what it said was true, that meant inquisitors coming to the school. And that was a thoroughly frightening thought. Mr. Crossley sighed and put the note in his pocket. “Right, everyone,” he said. “Put away your journals and get into line for music.”

As soon as 6B had shuffled away to the school hall, Mr. Crossley sped to the staff room, hoping to find someone he could consult about the note.

He was lucky enough to find Miss Hodge there. As Theresa Mullett and Estelle Green had observed, Mr. Crossley was in love with Miss Hodge. But of course he never let it show. Probably the one person in the school who did not seem to know was Miss Hodge herself. Miss Hodge was a small neat person who wore neat gray skirts and blouses and her hair was even neater and smoother than Theresa Mullett’s. She was busy making neat stacks of books on the staff room table, and she went on making them all the time Mr. Crossley was telling her excitedly about the note. She spared the note one glance.

“No, I can’t tell who wrote it either,” she said.

“But what shall I do about it?” Mr. Crossley pleaded. “Even if it’s true, it’s such a spiteful thing to write! And suppose it is true. Suppose one of them is—” He was in a pitiable state. He wanted so badly to attract Miss Hodge’s attention, but he knew that words like witch were not the kind of words one used in front of a lady. “I don’t like to say it in front of you.”

“I was brought up to be sorry for witches,” Miss Hodge remarked calmly.

“Oh, so was I! We all are,” Mr. Crossley said hastily. “I just wondered how I should handle it—”

Miss Hodge lined up another stack of books. “I think it’s just a silly joke,” she said. “Ignore it. Aren’t you supposed to be teaching 4C?”

“Yes, yes. I suppose I am,” Mr. Crossley agreed miserably. And he was forced to hurry away without Miss Hodge’s having looked at him once.

Miss Hodge thoughtfully squared off another stack of books, until she was sure Mr. Crossley had gone. Then she smoothed her smooth hair and hurried away upstairs to find Mr. Wentworth.

Mr. Wentworth, as deputy head, had a study where he wrestled with the schedules and various other problems Miss Cadwallader gave him. When Miss Hodge tapped on the door, he was wrestling with a particularly fierce one. There were seventy people in the school orchestra. Fifty of these were also in the school choir and twenty of those fifty were in the school play. Thirty boys in the orchestra were in various football teams, and twenty of the girls played hockey for the school. At least a third played basketball as well. The volleyball team were all in the school play. Problem: How do you arrange rehearsals and practices without asking most people to be in three places at once? Mr. Wentworth rubbed the thin patch at the back of his hair despairingly. “Come in,” he said. He saw the bright, smiling, anxious face of Miss Hodge, but his mind was not on her at all.

vocabulary.definition: Experiencing worry or unease about something uncertain or potentially bad.
vocabulary.example: The bright, smiling, anxious face of Miss Hodge.

vocabulary.definition: To cause interest or attention to oneself or itself.
vocabulary.example: He wanted so badly to attract Miss Hodge’s attention.

vocabulary.definition: In a quiet and peaceful manner.
vocabulary.example: Miss Hodge remarked calmly.

vocabulary.definition: To look quickly in a particular direction.
vocabulary.example: He cast a quick glance at the note.

vocabulary.definition: A group of people who sing together, often in church or school.
vocabulary.example: Fifty were also in the school choir.

vocabulary.definition: A habitual combination of words that sound natural to native speakers.
vocabulary.example: Meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations.

vocabulary.definition: To seek advice or information from someone, especially an expert or authoritative person.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley sped to the staff room, hoping to find someone he could consult about the note.

vocabulary.definition: The person who is second in command at a school.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Wentworth, as deputy head.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing loss of hope.
vocabulary.example: He rubbed the thin patch at the back of his hair despairingly.

vocabulary.definition: Showing great enthusiasm or eagerness.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley was telling her excitedly about the note.

vocabulary.definition: Having or displaying an intense or aggressive quality.
vocabulary.example: A particularly fierce problem.

vocabulary.definition: Causing fear or alarm.
vocabulary.example: That was a thoroughly frightening thought.

vocabulary.definition: A quick or brief look at something.
vocabulary.example: She spared the note one glance.

vocabulary.definition: With excessive speed or urgency; hurriedly.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley said hastily.

vocabulary.definition: To deliberately pay no attention to.
vocabulary.example: I think it’s just a silly joke, ignore it.

vocabulary.definition: People who conduct an official investigation or inquiry, often in a harsh or severe manner.
vocabulary.example: That meant inquisitors coming to the school.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows unhappiness or discomfort.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley agreed miserably.

vocabulary.definition: A large group of musicians who play various instruments together.
vocabulary.example: There were seventy people in the school orchestra.

vocabulary.definition: To a higher degree than usual; especially.
vocabulary.example: He was wrestling with a particularly fierce one.

vocabulary.definition: Deserving or arousing pity; lamentable.
vocabulary.example: He was in a pitiable state.

vocabulary.definition: To ask or appeal earnestly and emotionally.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley pleaded, “But what shall I do about it?”

vocabulary.definition: A practice session for a performance.
vocabulary.example: How do you arrange rehearsals and practices?

vocabulary.definition: To move slowly without lifting the feet properly.
vocabulary.example: 6B had shuffled away to the school hall.

vocabulary.definition: Showing a desire to harm, annoy, or frustrate someone deliberately.
vocabulary.example: It’s such a spiteful thing to write!

translations.ru: аккуратно выстроить, располагать в аккуратный ряд

vocabulary.definition: To arrange items neatly or in square shapes; to organize tidily.
vocabulary.example: Miss Hodge thoughtfully squared off another stack of books.

vocabulary.definition: A room in a school where teachers can meet and rest.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Crossley sped to the staff room.

vocabulary.definition: Completely, very much, in every way possible.
vocabulary.example: And that was a thoroughly frightening thought.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows careful thinking or consideration.
vocabulary.example: Miss Hodge thoughtfully squared off another stack of books.

vocabulary.definition: To struggle or fight with a difficult problem or challenge.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Wentworth wrestled with schedules and other problems.
